common_name: SunGold Cherry Tomato (Takii Seed Golden-Orange F1 Cherry)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; SunGold is an F1 hybrid cherry tomato from Takii Seed (Japan) widely considered the sweetest and most popular cherry tomato in American home gardens — the small, golden-orange fruits ripen early and continuously, offering exceptional sweetness with tropical-fruity overtones unlike any other cherry tomato.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun after last frost; SunGold is an indeterminate vine that benefits from strong staking or a tomato cage at least 5–6 feet tall; remove suckers for concentrated fruiting or allow to develop for maximum production; harvest when golden-orange and slightly soft. SunGold is an F1 hybrid cherry tomato developed by Takii Seed of Japan and first introduced to American gardeners in the early 1990s; it has since become the most widely grown and most frequently praised cherry tomato in American home gardens, consistently ranked first or near-first in taste comparisons by extension services, seed companies, and tomato enthusiasts for its exceptional sweetness and complex flavor. The small (3/4 to 1 inch), round to slightly oval fruits ripen from green through yellow to a distinctive rich golden-orange color; the flavor at peak ripeness is extraordinary — intensely sweet, with brix values of 9–10 or higher (far above standard tomato sweetness), and distinct tropical-fruity notes (mango, passion fruit, tangerine) that are unique among tomatoes and make SunGold recognizable in a blind taste test. The indeterminate vines are extremely productive, bearing prolific clusters of 15–30 fruits from early summer until frost; the fruits are crack-resistant for a cherry type. Not an open-pollinated heirloom (seed saved from SunGold will not come true), but the exceptional flavor makes it a standard in virtually every serious home garden.

common_name: Sweet Million Cherry Tomato (F1 Red Cluster Cherry)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant after last frost into full sun and fertile, well-drained soil; Sweet Million is a widely grown F1 red cherry tomato bearing prolific clusters of crack-resistant, uniformly sized 1-inch red fruits with excellent sweet flavor — an improvement on the classic Sweet 100 type with better crack resistance, disease resistance, and fruit quality for both home gardens and market growers.

directions: Transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; stake or cage early as Sweet Million produces vigorous indeterminate vines; the fruits ripen in large clusters of 15–30 and can be harvested cluster by cluster. Sweet Million is an F1 hybrid red cherry tomato developed as an improved successor to the classic Sweet 100 cherry tomato — retaining Sweet 100's exceptional productivity and sweet flavor while adding meaningfully better crack resistance, improved disease resistance (including resistance to fusarium and verticillium wilts), and more uniform fruit sizing than the original Sweet 100. The fruits are small, round, and uniformly 3/4 to 1 inch, ripening a brilliant red; the flavor is classic cherry tomato — sweet, high in sugar relative to acids, bright and fruity with good tomato character. The vines are indeterminate and extremely productive, bearing what appears to be a literally endless succession of clusters from midsummer through frost — the name 'Sweet Million' suggests the volume of fruit a well-grown plant produces. Sweet Million is an outstanding choice for home gardeners who want maximum production of reliable, sweet red cherry tomatoes for fresh eating, salads, and snacking; it is particularly recommended for humid climates (including the Gulf Coast) where crack-prone cherry varieties fail in summer rains. Disease-resistant foliage is an advantage in humid zone 8–10 conditions.

common_name: Matt's Wild Cherry Tomato (Wild-Type Tiny Red Cherry)
scientific_name: Solanum lycopersicum var. cerasiforme 'Matt's Wild Cherry'

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Matt's Wild Cherry is a variety collected from a wild or semi-wild population in the Hidalgo region of Mexico by Matt Liebman and introduced to the seed-saving community — it produces extremely small (pea-sized, 3/8–1/2 inch), intensely flavored red fruits with a bold, complex, acidic-sweet tomato flavor that recalls the taste of truly wild tomatoes and is unlike any commercial cherry tomato.

directions: Transplant into well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; Matt's Wild Cherry is an extremely vigorous grower requiring sturdy staking; the pea-sized fruits are prone to cracking when ripe and should be harvested frequently; self-sows readily in mild climates — may reappear without replanting. Matt's Wild Cherry is a tomato variety with an interesting origin: it was collected by Matt Liebman from a wild or semi-wild population (possibly a naturalized escape) in the Hidalgo state of Mexico — an area near the center of origin of the domestic tomato — and introduced to North American seed-saving networks through the Seed Savers Exchange and similar organizations. The variety is classified as Solanum lycopersicum var. cerasiforme, the wild cherry tomato type considered ancestral to all cultivated tomatoes, and it displays many wild-type characteristics: extremely small fruits (pea-sized, only 3/8 to 1/2 inch in diameter), prolific production (literally hundreds of fruits per plant), high seed-to-flesh ratio, thin skin prone to cracking, and exceptional flavor intensity that is quite different from cultivated cherry tomatoes. The flavor is extraordinarily complex for such small fruits — intensely tomato-ish, with high acidity balanced against good sweetness, and a deep, earthy, wild tomato character that reflects the original tomato flavor before centuries of breeding selected for larger, milder, less acidic fruits. For flavor enthusiasts and tomato purists, Matt's Wild Cherry offers a connection to the ancestral tomato that no commercial variety can provide.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Glacier is a notably early-maturing, cold-tolerant slicing tomato capable of setting fruit when nighttime temperatures fall to 45–50°F — ideal for short-season northern gardeners, high-altitude gardens, or Pacific Northwest coastal conditions where most tomatoes fail to set fruit in cool summers.

directions: Transplant into warm, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after last frost; Glacier is a determinate to semi-determinate variety that does not require extensive staking; it produces most of its fruit in a compressed season (excellent for northern and coastal climates) and can be grown without pruning. Glacier is an open-pollinated early-season slicing tomato specifically suited to cool-summer and short-season climates: it sets fruit at nighttime temperatures as low as 45–50°F, a capability that distinguishes it from most standard tomato varieties (which require consistent nighttime temperatures above 55°F for pollen viability and fruit set). This cold-setting ability makes Glacier valuable for Pacific Northwest coastal gardens where summer temperatures rarely exceed 75°F, high-altitude Rocky Mountain gardens with cool nights, and short-season northern gardens (zones 3–5) where the growing season is compressed. The medium-small (3–4 oz), round to slightly oblate, red fruits ripen early (55–65 days from transplant) and have good flavor for an early variety — pleasantly sweet-acidic balance with classic fresh tomato character; the flavor intensifies if warm conditions prevail at harvest time. The determinate to semi-determinate plants (36–48 inches) do not require the extensive staking that indeterminate varieties need, making them convenient for short-season production in smaller garden spaces.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Mt. Washington was selected and released for its ability to set fruit in cold conditions similar to those at the summit area of Mt. Washington in New Hampshire — one of the coldest and windiest spots in the eastern United States — making it among the earliest-producing cherry tomato varieties available and suitable for the most challenging cold-summer and short-season climates.

directions: Transplant into well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; Mt. Washington is a compact, determinate-type cherry tomato that does not require staking; the plants concentrate production in a relatively tight window (good for canning or preserving in quantity); harvest when fully red and slightly soft. Mt. Washington is an ultra-early cherry tomato selected specifically for cold-tolerance and short-season performance, named for Mt. Washington in New Hampshire — famous for its extreme weather (the site held the world record for the highest surface wind speed ever recorded at 231 mph in April 1934) and the notoriously harsh conditions of its summit area. The variety produces small, round, red cherry tomatoes (about 1 inch) with good, sweet-tart flavor in 52–60 days from transplant — one of the fastest-maturing cherry tomatoes available. The compact, determinate plants (24–36 inches) are appropriate for container growing and produce a compressed flush of fruit ideal for gardeners who want to can or preserve cherry tomatoes in quantity rather than harvest over an extended season. Cold tolerance allows fruit set at nighttime temperatures approaching 45°F, making this variety one of a handful suitable for truly short growing seasons with cold nights throughout the summer.
sources: University of New Hampshire Cooperative Extension
